The underlying gap — no erasure mechanism at all, no FK constraints to enforce referential integrity on manual deletes — is genuine and warrants the suggested fix.

passthrough_p2.claim: Refresh-token rotation re-issues but does NOT invalidate the previous refresh token, contradicting the docstring's claim that 'a leaked refresh becomes useless after one use'.
passthrough_p2.evidence: `refresh()` mints `new_refresh = issue_refresh_token(user)` and sets the cookie, but never bumps `user.token_version`, never persists the previous `jti` to a revocation list, and the old JWT continues to verify (same `tv`, signature still valid, exp not reached) until the 7-day TTL expires. An attacker who siphons a refresh cookie can keep redeeming it in parallel with the legitimate session for up to a week, undetected, until the user manually triggers /logout.
passthrough_p2.suggested_fix: Either persist `(user_id, jti)` of issued refresh tokens to a `refresh_tokens` table with `used_at` / `revoked_at`, or bump `user.token_version` on every successful /refresh (this invalidates the current access token too — caller already gets the new one in the response). The latter is the smaller diff; the former preserves multi-device sessions.

passthrough_p2.claim: CSP `script-src` includes `'unsafe-inline'` and `'unsafe-eval'` in production builds, so any XSS sink that reaches the page has full script execution. The protective intent of CSP is effectively voided.
passthrough_p2.evidence: `script-src 'self' 'unsafe-inline' 'unsafe-eval'` is emitted unconditionally. The trailing comment 'Next.js needs unsafe-eval in dev; tighten with nonces in prod build' acknowledges the intent but no `process.env.NODE_ENV` branching swaps to a stricter prod CSP. Compare to lines 43-45 where HSTS is gated on NODE_ENV — the same toggle was just not added here.
passthrough_p2.suggested_fix: Use Next.js 15 strict CSP with per-request nonces (https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/content-security-policy) — Next can inject the nonce into its own runtime scripts so `'unsafe-inline'` and `'unsafe-eval'` can be dropped in the production build. Minimum acceptable: drop `'unsafe-eval'` in prod (Next.js production bundles do not require eval).

passthrough_p2.claim: Combination of `uvicorn --forwarded-allow-ips=*` and slowapi `key_func=get_remote_address` lets any client bypass IP-based rate limits by setting `X-Forwarded-For` to a random value.
passthrough_p2.evidence: `--forwarded-allow-ips=*` instructs uvicorn to trust `X-Forwarded-For` from any source. `get_remote_address` (slowapi) then reads `request.client.host`, which uvicorn rewrites to the XFF value. An attacker can issue thousands of /auth/login attempts with `X-Forwarded-For: <random-ip>` per request, each starting from a fresh 120/min bucket. Combined with finding 6 (no per-endpoint login limit), this enables effectively-unbounded password brute force.
passthrough_p2.suggested_fix: If the API is behind a known proxy, set `--forwarded-allow-ips` to that proxy's IP only (e.g. `--forwarded-allow-ips=10.0.0.0/8`). If the API is direct-exposed, drop `--proxy-headers` entirely. Independently, validate slowapi keying by including request.headers['x-forwarded-for'] hash + remote_addr.

passthrough_p2.claim: POST /auth/login has no per-endpoint rate limit — only the global 120/min default — and no per-account lockout.
passthrough_p2.evidence: `upload_gazette` carries `@limiter.limit(_get_upload_limit)` (10/min), but `login()` has no `@limiter.limit(...)` decorator. With the bypass in finding 5, an attacker can iterate the full top-1000-passwords list against a single email well within the global default. The constant-time `_DUMMY_HASH` defends against email enumeration but not against credential stuffing.
passthrough_p2.suggested_fix: Add `@limiter.limit('5/minute;30/hour')` on /auth/login keyed by the submitted email (slowapi supports custom key_func per route). Also add per-account: lock the account or impose increasing delays after N consecutive failures in M minutes, stored in Redis.

passthrough_p2.claim: Sync redis client (`redis.Redis.from_url(...).ping()` / `.enqueue(...)`) used inside async FastAPI handlers — blocks event loop on every readiness probe and every upload.
passthrough_p2.evidence: main.py /health/ready: `r = Redis.from_url(get_settings().redis_url, socket_timeout=2); r.ping()` inside `async def ready`. gazettes.py upload: `Queue("ingest", connection=Redis.from_url(...))).enqueue(...)` inside `async def upload_gazette`. The 2 s socket_timeout on ping means a flaky Redis stalls the loop for 2 s per probe.
passthrough_p2.suggested_fix: Use `redis.asyncio` (aka `aioredis` since redis-py 4.2): `from redis.asyncio import Redis as ARedis; r = ARedis.from_url(...); await r.ping()`. For RQ enqueue, wrap in `await asyncio.to_thread(...)` since RQ doesn't have an async client.

passthrough_p2.claim: Filename whose first char isn't 'A' or 'B' silently maps to GazetteType.A. A B-file uploaded with a typo'd name (e.g. 'bulletin_T2_2026.pdf' starts with 'b' — OK, but 'gazette_B_T2.pdf' starts with 'g') becomes a type-A gazette, the parser uses (210) markers only, extracts 0 sections, and the gazette completes silently with row_count=0.
passthrough_p2.evidence: `parse_filename_meta` line 33: `letter = filename[:1].upper() if filename else 'A'`. Line 34: `gazette_type = GazetteType.B if letter == 'B' else GazetteType.A`. There's no fallback / error — anything not starting with B is silently A. Upload route accepts the result without validation. The CLAUDE.md residual stats won't notice because they assume gazette_type matches reality.
passthrough_p2.suggested_fix: Reject filenames whose first letter isn't [AaBb] with HTTP 400 in upload_gazette, OR require the user to explicitly select A/B in the upload form and stop inferring from filename at all. Returning `None` from parse_filename_meta and gating on it in the route is the smallest change.

passthrough_p2.claim: Uses deprecated `datetime.utcnow()` (timezone-naive) as fallback when no gazettes exist. In Python 3.12+ this is deprecated; the result is also naive, so when serialised it becomes ambiguous in the API response.
passthrough_p2.evidence: `last_sync = last.scalar_one_or_none() or datetime.utcnow()`. `Gazette.uploaded_at` is `DateTime(timezone=True)` so the DB path returns a tz-aware datetime; the fallback returns a tz-naive datetime. Pydantic serialises them differently (the naive one drops the tz suffix in JSON), and any consumer that diffs `lastSyncAt` against `datetime.now(tz.utc)` would silently get hours of skew when the fallback fires.
passthrough_p2.suggested_fix: Replace with `datetime.now(UTC)` (the project already imports `from datetime import UTC, datetime` elsewhere). The same import pattern is used correctly in worker/ingest.py:419 and auth.py:98.

passthrough_p2.claim: Regression baselines are stale and only detect regressions ABOVE the baseline. After fixes drove counts below baseline (e.g. madrid_number_in_applicant from 14 → 12), the script will mark a re-climb from 12 to 13 as 'no regression' because 13 < 14.
passthrough_p2.evidence: BASELINES dict: `"madrid_number_in_applicant": 14` but CLAUDE.md states `12 B rows have a Madrid registration number in (732) (was ~14)`. `"address_fragment_in_applicant": 7` but CLAUDE.md says `0 B rows have only an address fragment in (732)`. main() computes `delta = len(suspects) - baseline` and only flags regression when `delta > 0`, so any drift back upward toward the stale baseline is invisible.
passthrough_p2.suggested_fix: Update baselines to the current observed counts after each fix, OR change semantics: store last-known-good counts in a small JSON file that the audit reads + updates on `--blessed` runs. Simpler short-term fix: set `address_fragment_in_applicant = 0`, `madrid_number_in_applicant = 12` to match CLAUDE.md.

passthrough_p2.claim: Four MDX articles use `id="None"` on every <DocsH2>, producing duplicate IDs that violate HTML uniqueness and break screen-reader heading navigation.
passthrough_p2.evidence: api.mdx lines 17,22,40,44,58 — every DocsH2 has id="None". article-112.mdx lines 9,17,27 — same. image-search.mdx lines 10,23,36 — same. first-watchlist.mdx lines 9,13,21,25 — same. DocsH2 (components/marketing/docs/DocsH2.tsx:41) renders `<h2 id={id}>` so the page ships with 3-5 elements all having id="None". Looks like a copy-paste from a Python None default. None of these four articles have a DocsTOC so no anchor target is broken, but assistive tech (NVDA, JAWS, VoiceOver) reads them as unintended anchor targets.
passthrough_p2.suggested_fix: Replace id="None" with meaningful kebab-case ids in each file (e.g. api.mdx: id="auth", "search", "watchlists", "webhooks", "rate-limits"). Add ESLint rule or grep guard against `id="None"` in app/frontend/app/(marketing)/docs/_articles/.

passthrough_p2.claim: Unauthenticated visitors to any /(app)/ route get a visible flash of TopNav + the protected page before the redirect-to-login effect fires.
passthrough_p2.evidence: AuthProvider mounts in `(app)/layout.tsx` and renders children unconditionally (`<AuthProvider><CmdKProvider><TopNav /><main>{children}</main></CmdKProvider></AuthProvider>`). Inside AuthProvider, the loading→user check + router.replace lives in a useEffect that only runs after first commit. TodayPage starts its own data fetches in parallel, all 401, and only THEN the redirect happens. Net result: unauth user sees TopNav with a shimmer avatar + half-rendered Today page before being yanked to /login.
passthrough_p2.suggested_fix: In (app)/layout.tsx, render a blocking <Splash /> while `loading || !user` instead of {children}. Or move the auth check to a server component / Next.js middleware that redirects before any JSX streams.

passthrough_p2.claim: The `Gazettes` tab is shown to non-admin users. Clicking it flashes the admin page and bounces back to /today.
passthrough_p2.evidence: top-nav.tsx line 18 lists `{ href: '/admin/gazettes', label: 'Gazettes' }` in TABS with no role guard. admin/gazettes/page.tsx lines 32-40 calls `api.adminCheck()` on mount and `router.replace('/today')` if not admin. So a viewer clicks Gazettes → URL changes → 'Checking access…' renders → backend round-trip → bounce. UserMenu (top-nav.tsx:107-113) already reads user.role for the avatar but doesn't use it to filter tabs.
passthrough_p2.suggested_fix: Filter TABS by role: `TABS.filter(t => t.href !== '/admin/gazettes' || user?.role === 'admin')`. Keep the server gate as defense-in-depth.

passthrough_p2.claim: All modal/dialog overlays lack `aria-modal="true"`, focus traps, and inert backgrounds — keyboard users can tab out of the open dialog into the page behind.
passthrough_p2.evidence: cmdk.tsx:219 `<div ... role="dialog" aria-label="Command palette">` — no aria-modal, no focus management beyond the initial input focus, no inert on backdrop. Tabbing past the last item escapes the palette. CreateModal (watchlists:238) and CSVImportModal (watchlists:482) use raw `<div onClick={onClose}>` backdrops with no role="dialog" at all, no aria-labelledby, no focus restoration on close. TweaksPanel (tweaks-panel.tsx:91) has role="dialog" + aria-label but same focus-trap gap.
passthrough_p2.suggested_fix: Adopt Radix `<Dialog>` or @react-aria's `useModal` for all four. At minimum: add `aria-modal="true"`, focus-trap (Tab cycles inside the panel), restore focus to the trigger on close, mark page content inert (`inert` attr) while open.

passthrough_p2.claim: Watchlists CSV import parser has documented bugs (CRLF in quoted fields breaks it) and no upload size guard — a multi-MB paste freezes the tab.
passthrough_p2.evidence:
watchlists/page.tsx:345-346 the comment itself states: "Doesn't handle CR/LF inside quoted fields". Line 348: `text.replace(/
?/g, "
").split("
").filter((l) => l.length > 0)` — splits on newline BEFORE quote-state tracking, so any quoted multi-line cell (common in Vietnamese mark descriptions) corrupts the row. Line 387: a row missing only the name silently becomes a warning, but a multi-line cell would generate header-misaligned warnings the user can't diagnose. The submit loop (458-472) is sequential — 1000-row CSV makes 1000 sequential POSTs blocking on each, with no batch endpoint and no Promise.allSettled concurrency cap. No file size limit before reader.readAsText (line 437) — a 200MB CSV freezes the renderer thread.
passthrough_p2.suggested_fix: Replace parseCSV with PapaParse (already-tested, handles quoted newlines + chunks). Cap file size at e.g. 5MB before reading. Cap inline rows at 500 with a warning. Add server-side `POST /watchlists/bulk` and call it once instead of N times.

passthrough_p2.claim: MarkSpecimen `` lacks `loading="lazy"`, `decoding="async"`, and explicit width/height; 50 result-grid specimens load immediately, hurting Search LCP and bandwidth.
passthrough_p2.evidence: components/specimen/index.tsx lines 111-128 render `` with no loading attribute. ResultsGrid (search/results-grid.tsx:51) wraps MarkSpecimen inside a 50-card grid per page. Each PNG is hit-of-disk at backend `/static/image///.png` — typically 30-80KB each, 50 × 50 = 2.5MB on initial paint. Next/image was deliberately avoided per the comment in specimen/index.tsx for the dynamic backend URLs, but loading=lazy alone is a one-line fix.
passthrough_p2.suggested_fix: Add `loading="lazy"` (or `loading="eager"` only for above-the-fold first row) + `decoding="async"` to the `` in MarkSpecimen. Pass through width/height as attrs (not just style) so the browser can compute layout pre-load.

passthrough_p2.claim: Compare page non-null-asserts `find()` results twice — if backend returns scores referencing a deleted/missing mark, page crashes with TypeError instead of showing a graceful error.
passthrough_p2.evidence: compare/page.tsx line 61: `const anchor = data.marks.find((m) => m.id === data.anchorId)!;` — `!` masks the undefined case. Line 295 in ScorecardBand: `const m = marks.find((x) => x.id === s.markId)!;` — same. If the user calls /compare?ids=A,B,C and B has been deleted between Search and Compare, the backend may return marks=[A,C] and scores=[{markId:B,...}] (depending on backend behavior). The find() returns undefined; TypeScript is happy with `!`; runtime throws on the next property access. No error boundary catches it (the error state at line 51 only handles the catch from `api.compare`).
passthrough_p2.suggested_fix: Replace `!` with explicit guards: `if (!anchor) { setError('Anchor mark not found'); return; }`. Same for ScorecardBand. Add a top-level ErrorBoundary so any future find()! pattern degrades to an error UI instead of a white screen.

passthrough_p2.claim: TweaksPanel (dev-only theme/density/serif knobs) is rendered for every authenticated user in production. Its file header literally calls it 'runtime theming knobs'.
passthrough_p2.evidence: (app)/layout.tsx line 31 mounts `<TweaksPanel />` unconditionally. tweaks-panel.tsx file header (lines 1-15) describes it as a theme/density tweaker referenced from the 'design handoff README'. No NODE_ENV check, no role check. End users see a Sliders floating button bottom-right of every in-app page.
passthrough_p2.suggested_fix: Wrap with `{process.env.NODE_ENV !== 'production' && <TweaksPanel />}` in (app)/layout.tsx. Or gate via role=admin if it's meant to be a real customer feature.

passthrough_p2.claim: Content Security Policy ships `'unsafe-eval'` in prod despite the comment promising it would be dev-only.
passthrough_p2.evidence: next.config.mjs line 22: `"script-src 'self' 'unsafe-inline' 'unsafe-eval'"` with inline comment `// Next.js needs unsafe-eval in dev; tighten with nonces in prod build`. No `process.env.NODE_ENV === 'production'` branch follows. The HSTS header at lines 43-45 has the conditional, proving the pattern is understood — but the unsafe-eval one was never split out. Combined with 'unsafe-inline' for both script and style, this CSP gives essentially no protection.
passthrough_p2.suggested_fix: Split CSP by env: production drops 'unsafe-eval' and uses Next.js nonces for inline scripts. If full nonce migration is too much, at minimum remove 'unsafe-eval' in prod and verify the build still works.

passthrough_p2.claim: Every result card / row contains TWO separate `<Link>` elements pointing to the same `/marks/<id>` URL — screen readers announce duplicated tab stops for the same destination.
passthrough_p2.evidence: results-grid.tsx line 50 (image link wrapping MarkSpecimen) and line 61 (text link wrapping the name/meta). Both point to `/marks/${m.id}`. results-table.tsx line 55 (specimen link) + line 66 (name link). Tab key visits both. axe-core flags this as 'duplicated link names'. WCAG 2.4.4 (Link Purpose) is borderline because the text differs, but the duplication is undeniable UX noise — particularly painful with 50 cards × 2 links.
passthrough_p2.suggested_fix: Wrap the entire card body in one `<Link>` and use `<button onClick stopPropagation>` for the checkbox toggle so the link captures all card surface. Or use a single Link with aria-label and visually-hidden surface around the card.

passthrough_p2.claim: Copy-link button crashes silently in non-secure contexts where `navigator.clipboard` is undefined.
passthrough_p2.evidence: marks/[id]/page.tsx line 441: `<Button variant="tiny" onClick={() => navigator.clipboard.writeText(window.location.href)}>Copy link</Button>`. No feature detect, no fallback to `document.execCommand('copy')`, no toast confirmation. If the app is ever served over plain HTTP (staging without TLS) or inside an iframe without clipboard-write permission, click throws TypeError or rejects with no user feedback.
passthrough_p2.suggested_fix: Guard with `if (!navigator.clipboard) { /* fallback */ }`, then show a toast on success / error. Or use a battle-tested helper like react-use-clipboard.

passthrough_p2.claim: Marketing landing CTAs use plain `<a href>` for internal navigation, forcing full page reloads instead of client-side route transitions.
passthrough_p2.evidence: page.tsx line 162: `<a href="/login" className="btn btn-primary btn-lg">`. Line 510: `<a href="/login" ...>`. Line 511: `<a href="/pricing" ...>`. MarketingNav (line 55-60) and pricing/page.tsx (line 186) DO use `<Link>` correctly, proving the pattern is known — the landing page just missed three. Result: clicking Hero CTA on / unmounts every component, refetches every font, re-runs the AuthProvider's `/auth/refresh` round-trip even though the user has zero need for it.
passthrough_p2.suggested_fix: Replace the three `<a>` tags with `<Link>` from next/link.

passthrough_p2.claim: `ix_gazettes_sha256` btree duplicates the index automatically created by `uq_gazettes_sha256 UNIQUE`. Both cover the same column; one is dead weight that costs write amplification on every gazette insert.
passthrough_p2.evidence: `\d gazettes` shows both `uq_gazettes_sha256 UNIQUE CONSTRAINT, btree (sha256)` AND `ix_gazettes_sha256 btree (sha256)`. pg_stat_user_indexes confirms `ix_gazettes_sha256` has been used 22 times but the UNIQUE index `uq_gazettes_sha256` has 0 scans — Postgres prefers the unique index. Init migration creates both: `op.create_index("ix_gazettes_sha256", ...)` then `sa.UniqueConstraint("sha256", ...)`. The model declares `sha256: Mapped[str] = mapped_column(..., index=True)` AND `__table_args__ = (UniqueConstraint("sha256", ...))`, producing both.
passthrough_p2.suggested_fix: Migration: `op.drop_index('ix_gazettes_sha256', table_name='gazettes')` and remove `index=True` from the model's `sha256` column declaration (the UniqueConstraint already provides the index).

passthrough_p2.claim: The partial WHERE clause `WHERE applicant_name IS NOT NULL` and `WHERE mark_sample IS NOT NULL` on the trigram indexes provides essentially zero size benefit. Only 3 of 46k rows have NULL applicant_name; ~12k of 46k rows have NULL mark_sample (the partial is moderately useful for mark_sample but trivial for applicant_name).
passthrough_p2.evidence: `SELECT COUNT(*) FROM trademarks WHERE applicant_name IS NULL` → 3. The applicant_name partial WHERE excludes 3 of 46758 rows = 0.006%. The partial clause forces planner to verify `applicant_name IS NOT NULL` is implied by the user's WHERE, which it always is when querying ILIKE (NULL never matches ILIKE), so no real cost — but the doc claim that the partial saves space is wrong for applicant_name. The mark_sample partial does meaningfully reduce index size (26% NULL rows excluded).
passthrough_p2.suggested_fix: Drop the `postgresql_where=text("applicant_name IS NOT NULL")` on ix_trademarks_applicant_name_trgm (no meaningful benefit). Keep it for mark_sample. Not urgent — current behavior is correct, just under-documented in the rationale comment.

passthrough_p2.claim: Two declared btree indexes have effectively zero use in production access patterns: ix_trademarks_applicant_city (5 scans lifetime) and ix_trademarks_applicant_type (1 scan).
passthrough_p2.evidence: pg_stat_user_indexes: `ix_trademarks_applicant_city idx_scan=5`, `ix_trademarks_applicant_type idx_scan=1`. applicant_type has 2 cardinality values (Personal | Company) — btree on a binary column is always cheaper as a seq scan. applicant_city has ~5k distinct values across 47k rows; if the filter is rarely used, the index size (968kB) + write cost is wasted. By contrast, ix_trademarks_applicant_name (1340 scans) and ix_trademarks_application_number (29315 scans) are heavily used.
passthrough_p2.suggested_fix: If the search-page applicant_type facet is genuinely high-traffic, keep it (the index will warm up). Otherwise drop ix_trademarks_applicant_type and ix_trademarks_applicant_city. Confirm by running pg_stat_user_indexes after a few weeks of real use.

passthrough_p2.claim: `/api/v1/findings` and `_findings_summary` execute one SQL query per watchlist (`_findings_for_watchlist`). With M watchlists per user, response time grows linearly. Combined with the missing user-scoping (P1 above), this is M*N queries on a busy dashboard.
passthrough_p2.evidence: today.py:149 `for w in watchlists:` then `rows = await _findings_for_watchlist(session, w, per_list=3)` which runs one SELECT per watchlist (L134-140). At today's seed data (0 watchlists) this is invisible. At 50 watchlists/user, each digest request fires 51 SQL roundtrips serially.
passthrough_p2.suggested_fix: Rewrite `_findings_summary` as one query: build a WHERE clause UNION ALL'd across all watchlist queries, or use a CTE that joins watchlists to trademarks with a lateral subquery. Or precompute findings on each gazette ingest (the watchlist row already caches `total_count` / `new_count`).

passthrough_p2.claim: QueuePool with `pool_size=20, max_overflow=10` per uvicorn worker can exhaust Postgres connections under modest deployment.
passthrough_p2.evidence: Postgres `SHOW max_connections;` = 100 (default). Each uvicorn worker holds up to 30 connections (20 base + 10 overflow). A typical 4-worker uvicorn launch with the RQ worker process (sync engine, default pool_size=5+10=15) = 4*30 + 15 = 135 connections, > 100. The sync engine in worker/ingest.py (line 95) uses the default pool (5+10) so its share is bounded, but the API side dominates. Documentation in session.py:10-11 promises this is "safe and necessary for real traffic" but doesn't reckon with Postgres' default cap.
passthrough_p2.suggested_fix: Either lower pool_size to ~8-10 per worker (matches 4 workers × 18 = 72 < 100), document the assumed Postgres `max_connections` (raise to 200 in deploy), or front Postgres with PgBouncer in transaction mode.

passthrough_p2.claim: tm_name_index has no link back to `trademarks` and no provenance column on `trademarks.mark_sample` recording that the value came from the index vs. the PDF parser. Re-running the loader with a stale CSV would silently clobber any rows whose mark_sample was originally parsed from PDF if the CSV value differs — only saved today by the `WHERE mark_sample IS NULL OR mark_sample = ''` guard, which has no `enriched_at`/`source` audit trail.
passthrough_p2.evidence: models.py:333 `mark_sample: Mapped[str]` on TmNameIndex; trademarks.mark_sample has no source column. enrich_mark_samples.py:88-95 UPDATE only matches rows where existing mark_sample is NULL/empty, so today the guard is sufficient. But if a future operator runs `UPDATE trademarks SET mark_sample = NULL WHERE ...` to refresh from a new CSV, all PDF-parsed wordmarks would be lost. The provenance comment at models.py:13 says "The reference table stays around after enrichment" but no column on trademarks records this. The migration's downgrade is `DROP TABLE IF EXISTS tm_name_index CASCADE` — losing the source-of-truth for ~46k rows of `mark_sample` data, with no easy way to identify which rows came from the index.
passthrough_p2.suggested_fix: Add `mark_sample_source: Mapped[str | None] = mapped_column(String(16), nullable=True)` with values `pdf|noip_index|manual` set by the parser and the enrich script. Or add a `enriched_from_noip_at` timestamp. Not a current bug, but a P2 quality-debt item that will compound once multiple enrichment sources exist.

passthrough_p2.claim: Every GitHub Action is pinned to a mutable tag (@v6, @v4, @v0.36.0) rather than a 40-char SHA. A maintainer/repo compromise of `actions/checkout`, `pnpm/action-setup`, `aquasecurity/trivy-action`, or `github/codeql-action` could silently inject a malicious step into every CI run — including a step that exfiltrates GITHUB_TOKEN, reads repo secrets, or tampers with the SBOM upload.
passthrough_p2.evidence: ci.yml:41 `uses: actions/checkout@v6`, :86 `uses: pnpm/action-setup@v4`, :179 `uses: aquasecurity/trivy-action@v0.36.0`, :221 `uses: github/codeql-action/upload-sarif@v4`, :239 `uses: actions/upload-artifact@v7`, etc. None are SHA-pinned. GitHub's own published guidance for security-sensitive workflows is to pin third-party (and arguably first-party) actions to a full SHA and let Dependabot bump them.
passthrough_p2.suggested_fix: Convert to SHA-pinned references: `actions/checkout@<40-char-sha> # v6.x.y`. Dependabot already updates github-actions (dependabot.yml:85), so the maintenance burden is one merged PR per month. Most critical for `aquasecurity/trivy-action` (drives the security gate) and `github/codeql-action/upload-sarif` (touches Security tab).

passthrough_p2.claim: No workflow-level `permissions:` block declared. Only the `image-scan` job has a per-job permissions stanza. The other four jobs (backend, frontend, audit, e2e) inherit the default GITHUB_TOKEN scope, which for the repo's settings could grant `contents: write` and other elevated scopes a CI lint/test job has no need for.
passthrough_p2.evidence: ci.yml:160 is the ONLY `permissions:` block. backend/frontend/audit/e2e jobs declare none. `grep -nE '^permissions' .github/workflows/ci.yml` returns only line 160 (inside image-scan, indented). Without a workflow-default of `permissions: contents: read`, a compromised step (e.g. via the SHA-pinning gap above) could push to the repo.
passthrough_p2.suggested_fix: Add at the top of ci.yml: `permissions: contents: read` and override per-job only where writes are needed (image-scan already does this correctly with security-events: write).

passthrough_p2.claim: Postgres and Redis base images are pulled by floating tag (`postgres:16-alpine`, `redis:7-alpine`) and are never scanned by Trivy. Only the custom backend image is scanned. The two data-plane images are the highest-blast-radius attack surface (DB credential exfiltration, cache poisoning) and they get zero CVE gating.
passthrough_p2.evidence: ci.yml:151-243 `image-scan` job runs Trivy only on `tradenet-backend:ci`. No Trivy invocation against `postgres:16-alpine` or `redis:7-alpine`. Tags are mutable: a new `postgres:16-alpine` push could ship a regressed CVE between dev runs.
passthrough_p2.suggested_fix: (a) Pin postgres/redis to digest (`postgres:16-alpine@sha256:…`) in both docker-compose.yml and ci.yml services blocks. (b) Add a Trivy `image-ref` scan pass over each pinned data-plane digest in CI. Dependabot's docker ecosystem will keep them current.

passthrough_p2.claim: The Dockerfile HEALTHCHECK probes `/health` (pure liveness — returns ok unconditionally) rather than `/health/ready` (which actually checks DB and Redis connectivity). For docker-compose / Swarm deployments without a separate readiness probe, a container with broken DB connectivity will report 'healthy' indefinitely — load balancers will route traffic to it and every request will 500.
passthrough_p2.evidence: Dockerfile:53 `CMD curl --fail --silent http://localhost:8000/health || exit 1`. api/main.py:159-162: `async def health() -> dict: return {'status':'ok'}` — zero dependency checks. The real readiness handler at main.py:164-188 (`/health/ready`) actually pings DB + Redis and returns `degraded` on failure. DEPLOYMENT.md:94-102 correctly distinguishes them for k8s, but the Dockerfile-embedded HEALTHCHECK is what compose/Swarm/Nomad use.
passthrough_p2.suggested_fix: Either (a) change Dockerfile HEALTHCHECK to hit `/health/ready` and parse the JSON status field, or (b) document explicitly in DEPLOYMENT.md that compose deployments need a separate readiness gate (and that HEALTHCHECK is liveness-only). (a) is safer for non-k8s deploys.

passthrough_p2.claim: Rollback story relies on `alembic downgrade <revision>` (DEPLOYMENT.md:77) but downgrades are never exercised in CI, and several migrations have destructive downgrades (e.g. init drops trademarks + gazettes tables; 0004 drops watchlists; 0006 drops logo_path column). A botched rollback after a bad release silently deletes all production data. Forward-only migrations OR tested downgrades is the standard — this is neither.
passthrough_p2.evidence: ci.yml:64-66 runs `alembic upgrade head` only. No `alembic downgrade` step. `20260520_0001_init.py:125-126` does `op.drop_table('trademarks'); op.drop_table('gazettes')`. `20260520_0004_watchlists.py:41` drops watchlists. `20260521_0006_logo_path.py:29` drops the logo_path column. DEPLOYMENT.md:137 already hints at this risk: 'If watchlists' query JSONB changed shape, do not roll back schema — instead, run a forward fix.'
passthrough_p2.suggested_fix: Either (a) commit to forward-only migrations and explicitly remove the `alembic downgrade -1` instruction from DEPLOYMENT.md:77 (substitute: restore-from-backup), or (b) add a CI job that runs `alembic upgrade head && alembic downgrade -1 && alembic upgrade head` on every migration-touching PR. Today's docs promise a rollback path that is untested and destructive.

passthrough_p2.claim: Backend container image is not signed and has no SLSA/in-toto provenance attestation. SBOM is generated (sbom.cdx.json) and uploaded as an artifact (retention 90d) but there is no cosign signing, no `--provenance=true` buildx flag, no keyless signing via OIDC. A consumer of the image (k8s, compose, or a future customer) has no cryptographic way to verify it came from this CI run.
passthrough_p2.evidence: ci.yml:167-243 — `docker build` is plain BuildKit (no `--provenance`, no `--sbom` flag on the build). No `cosign sign` step. No `actions/attest-build-provenance` step (GitHub's first-party SLSA L3 action). SBOM is built post-hoc by Trivy, not bound to the image digest.
passthrough_p2.suggested_fix: Add `actions/attest-build-provenance@v2` after the docker build step (GitHub-hosted, OIDC-backed, ~3 lines of YAML). For images destined for a registry, also add a `cosign sign --yes <registry>/<image>@<digest>` step. SBOM should attach to the registry as a referrer, not just an artifact.

passthrough_p2.claim: Dev/CI POSTGRES_PASSWORD default is `tm` (same as user, same as db). docker-compose.yml gates this behind `${POSTGRES_PASSWORD:-tm}` so an operator who copies the file to a shared/staging host without overrides ships an open database. There is no startup validator on the Postgres side that rejects this default.
passthrough_p2.evidence: docker-compose.yml:11 `POSTGRES_PASSWORD: ${POSTGRES_PASSWORD:-tm}`. ci.yml:22,259 hardcode `POSTGRES_PASSWORD: tm`. Compose file has no comment warning operators that the default is unsafe outside CI (despite a top-of-file comment at line 1 about secret mgmt — this still gets copied around without overrides).
passthrough_p2.suggested_fix: (a) Drop the `:-tm` default entirely — require POSTGRES_PASSWORD to be set explicitly even in dev. Operators add it to `.env` once; CI sets it in the job env. (b) Mirror the TM_SECRET_KEY validator pattern: at backend startup, if `database_url` contains `tm:tm@` AND TM_ENV not in _SAFE_DEV_ENVS, raise.

passthrough_p2.location: app/backend/api/routes/*.py (none log) and no access-log middleware
passthrough_p2.claim: There is no HTTP access log and no per-request timing — the API surfaces no record of which endpoints were hit, response status, or latency, so debugging a 'this query is slow' or 'who deleted this watchlist' report is forensically impossible.
passthrough_p2.evidence: `grep -rn 'logger\|logging\|getLogger' app/backend/api/routes/*.py` returns nothing — not a single route logs anything. `RequestIDMiddleware` in `api/errors.py:29` binds a request_id but never emits a log line on request start/end. `grep -rn 'duration\|elapsed\|time\.perf' app/backend/api` shows zero timing instrumentation. Uvicorn's default access logger is the only thing producing access logs, and after `logging_config.py:52` does `root.handlers.clear()` it routes through structlog — but uvicorn access lines come from `uvicorn.access` at INFO level only if `--access-log` is passed (Dockerfile CMD does not pass it). The Prometheus instrumentator captures `http_request_duration_seconds`, but only when `enable_prometheus=True` and the operator has a Prom scrape configured — no log-based fallback.
passthrough_p2.suggested_fix: Add an HTTP-access middleware that logs `{method, path, status, duration_ms, request_id, user_id}` at request end via structlog. Even 5 lines of `BaseHTTPMiddleware.dispatch` covering `t0 = time.perf_counter(); response = await call_next(request); logger.info('request', method=..., path=..., status=response.status_code, duration_ms=...)` would give operators a baseline. Pass `--access-log` to uvicorn in the Dockerfile as a stopgap.

passthrough_p2.location: app/backend/worker/ — no RQ metrics, queue depth or failed-job exporter
passthrough_p2.claim: RQ queue depth, in-flight jobs, retry counts, and failed-registry size are not observable at all — operators can't see the ingest backlog, can't alert on stuck jobs, and can't tell when retries are hammering a flaky PDF.
passthrough_p2.evidence: `grep -rn 'rq_dashboard\|JobStatus\|queue_depth\|failed_job' app/backend` returns nothing. The Prometheus instrumentator (`api/main.py:194`) only instruments HTTP — it does not export RQ-specific metrics. `DEPLOYMENT.md:112` says 'RQ queue depth (scrape from Redis directly)' as future work but no Redis-scrape exporter is configured. RQ's default `FailedJobRegistry` retains failed jobs (gazettes.py:157 sets `failure_ttl=86400 * 7`) but there is no UI, no API endpoint, no alert. A worker dying mid-job leaves the gazette stuck in `status=processing` (ingest.py:344 logs a warning on the *next* attempt), but until someone manually re-queues, the row is wedged with no signal to operators.
passthrough_p2.suggested_fix: Add `rq-exporter` (Python Prometheus exporter for RQ) to the worker docker image, expose port 9726, scrape into the same Prometheus. Alert on `rq_jobs_failed_count > 0` and `rq_jobs_queue_size{queue='ingest'} > 5 for 10m`. Optionally mount `rq-dashboard` behind admin auth at `/admin/rq` so the on-call can re-queue without SSH.

passthrough_p2.claim: Backend errors lose their `request_id` correlation in the frontend — the error message is shown but the request_id needed to find the server-side log line is discarded.
passthrough_p2.evidence: `json()` parses `body?.error?.message ?? body?.detail` (line 143) but ignores `body.error.request_id`, which the backend (`api/errors.py:_envelope`) does include. A user reporting 'I got an error' has no way to give support a correlation ID. The X-Request-ID response header is also discarded by `json()` before `r.json()`.
passthrough_p2.suggested_fix: In `lib/api.ts:json()`, attach `request_id = r.headers.get('X-Request-ID') ?? body?.error?.request_id` to the thrown Error (e.g. via a custom ApiError class with a `requestId` field). Surface it in the user-facing toast: 'Error (ref: a1b2c3d4)'. Then operators can `grep request_id=a1b2c3d4` in structured logs.

passthrough_p2.claim: Authentication events — failed logins, token refresh attempts, password resets — are not logged anywhere, so brute-force attacks and credential-stuffing campaigns are invisible.
passthrough_p2.evidence: `grep -rn 'logger\|logging' app/backend/api/auth.py app/backend/api/routes/auth.py` returns nothing. Rate limits in `settings.py:57` (`rate_limit_default='120/minute'`) provide some protection but no log emission means an attacker hitting the limit just sees 429 and operators see nothing. SECURITY review depends on observability; without it, the only signal of 'is someone trying to break in?' is `slowapi` returning 429s, which themselves are not surfaced beyond a one-line response.
passthrough_p2.suggested_fix: Add `structlog.get_logger('auth')` and emit `logger.info('auth.login.success', user_id=...)`, `logger.warning('auth.login.failed', email_hash=..., ip=request.client.host)`, `logger.warning('auth.refresh.failed', reason=...)`. Hash or truncate the email so log search still works but the raw PII doesn't sit in logs forever. Set up an alert on `auth.login.failed` rate > N/min from a single IP.

passthrough_p2.claim: When `ingest_pdf` fails on a specific PDF, operators get the gazette filename and a one-line exception, but no per-section progress, no row-count-when-it-died, no wall-clock duration — the 'which section made it crash' question requires re-running with extra prints.
passthrough_p2.evidence: The success path logs only `'Ingested %s rows from %s'` (line 424). The failure path logs only `'Ingest failed for gazette %s'` with exc_info. There is no `logger.info('progress', rows_so_far=N, last_marker=...)` inside the `for section in processor.extract_records(...)` loop (line 401). There is no `time.perf_counter()` start/end, so 'this gazette took 23 minutes' is not in logs. If the extractor crashes on section #500 of a 1500-section PDF, the operator has no idea whether it crashed on 5, 500, or 1499 — they have to re-run with a debugger. Image extraction is similarly opaque: success logs `extracted_pngs` count (line 191), but failure logs only the directory path without telling you how many PNGs had been written before the crash.
passthrough_p2.suggested_fix: (a) Wrap the ingest body in `t0 = time.perf_counter()` and log `duration_ms` on completion. (b) Inside the loop, log progress every N sections: `if row_count % 500 == 0: logger.info('ingest.progress', gazette_id=..., rows=row_count)`. (c) In the except handler, bind the last-seen section's primary marker (`(210)`/`(111)`/`(116)`) so the failure log says 'crashed after processing section (111)=4-2026-00734'. These three changes turn 'sorry I have no idea what broke' into a one-look fix.

passthrough_p2.claim: No e2e test exercises marketing-page routing or the MarketingNav: there's no assertion that the nav links on /, /pricing, /coverage, /docs actually work, that the (marketing) layout doesn't accidentally mount AuthProvider (which would force-redirect anonymous traffic), or that internal anchor links inside docs MDX resolve.
passthrough_p2.evidence: search.spec.ts:14 comments 'the marketing landing renders MarketingNav with no Cmd-K search box' as a reason to navigate to /today instead — but doesn't verify MarketingNav itself. Login.spec.ts:13 confirms /today bounces to /login but doesn't verify /pricing or /coverage stay accessible anonymously. A regression where (marketing) layout starts requiring auth would break the entire public site without failing any current test.
passthrough_p2.suggested_fix: Add tests/e2e/marketing.spec.ts (anonymous storage state) that visits each marketing route (/, /pricing, /coverage, /docs, /docs/getting-started) and (a) asserts no /login redirect, (b) clicks the MarketingNav 'Pricing' / 'Docs' links and verifies destination URLs, (c) ensures the 'Sign in' CTA goes to /login.

passthrough_p2.claim: Local Playwright suite uses a literal hardcoded password ('playwright-test-password') that is created out-of-band by `python -m scripts.create_user`. If the user re-runs create_user with --password 'foo' for any reason (manual debugging, doc-following), or if a password-rotation policy lands, every local Playwright run silently fails at the auth.setup step with the same generic 'incorrect' error the test for bad-password assertion expects. The CI path overrides via env so CI is safe, but the local dev-loop is brittle.
passthrough_p2.evidence: auth.setup.ts:20 `const ADMIN_PASSWORD = process.env.PLAYWRIGHT_ADMIN_PASSWORD ?? 'playwright-test-password';`. create_user.py overrides user records on re-run (per its docstring 'Re-running with an existing email updates the user (rotates password, changes role)'), so any dev who runs create_user with a different password silently breaks Playwright. There is no health-check on setup that distinguishes 'password drift' from 'app down'.
passthrough_p2.suggested_fix: auth.setup.ts should detect a 401 specifically and emit an actionable error: 'Test admin password mismatch — re-run `python -m scripts.create_user --email playwright@local --password playwright-test-password` or set PLAYWRIGHT_ADMIN_PASSWORD'. Alternatively, make auth.setup.ts auto-bootstrap the user via a backend admin endpoint if the env exposes a setup token, removing the manual create_user step entirely.

passthrough_p2.claim: Token-expiry behavior is not tested at all. The auth module sets `exp` claims on both access and refresh tokens (auth.py:115, 132) but no test verifies that an expired access token returns 401, that a refresh token past its TTL is rejected, or that the clock-skew tolerance (if any) behaves correctly. A bug shipping that disables the `exp` validation in jose.decode would silently turn every JWT into a permanent credential — undetected.
passthrough_p2.evidence: `grep -n 'expired|exp_minutes|ACCESS_TOKEN_EXPIRE' app/backend/tests/test_auth.py` returns zero. test_auth.py covers bad password, unknown email, inactive user, logout-bumping-token-version, but nothing exercises the temporal dimension. The settings model presumably has an access_token_ttl knob (need to inspect) but no test passes a backdated `exp` claim or freezes time.
passthrough_p2.suggested_fix: Add test_expired_access_token_returns_401 that mints a token with `exp` set to (now - 60s) using the same secret_key and assert /auth/me returns 401. Add test_expired_refresh_cookie_returns_401 doing the same for /auth/refresh. Use freezegun or just construct the JWT manually with a backdated claim.

passthrough_p2.claim: Both audit scripts are documented as the canonical regression-detection harness for data-quality issues ('Each check reports count vs documented baseline + delta — delta > 0 flags a regression'). Yet neither script has any unit test. A breaking change to a SQL check (e.g., changing a LIKE pattern that no longer matches the Madrid-number-in-applicant case) would silently mask regressions — the script would still run clean but stop detecting the issue it was built to detect.
passthrough_p2.evidence: `grep -r 'audit_fields|audit_logos' app/backend/tests/` returns zero. CLAUDE.md states the residual counts (e.g., '12 B rows have a Madrid registration number in (732) (was ~14)') ARE the baseline these checks track. If a future tightening of the SQL check accidentally relaxes match criteria, the count drops to zero and the audit reports 'CLEAN' while the real-world data is no better — the worst possible failure mode.
passthrough_p2.suggested_fix: Add fixture-based tests: seed 5 known-bad rows matching each audit pattern (one row per pattern), run each audit check, assert each detects exactly the seeded row. This protects against false-negative drift in the audit regexes.

passthrough_p2.claim: No property-based testing (hypothesis) anywhere in the suite. The extractor and similarity engine are both ideal candidates: the extractor's marker regexes operate over an effectively infinite input space (every NOIP gazette line variation), and the similarity composite has invariants that should hold under all weight permutations (sum-to-1, verdict monotonicity, threshold-band boundaries).
passthrough_p2.evidence: `grep -r hypothesis app/backend/` returns no matches (excluding node_modules). test_similarity.py:316 manually asserts `sum(DEFAULT_WEIGHTS.values()) == pytest.approx(1.0)` for the single default — but per-matter weights from test_composite_per_matter_weights_override could violate this and no test would catch it. The extractor's marker patterns are all hand-tested with a few cases; an INID code arriving with unusual whitespace, mixed punctuation, or Vietnamese diacritics in unexpected positions has no fuzz coverage.
passthrough_p2.suggested_fix: For similarity: add hypothesis tests that (a) generate random weight dicts summing to 1 and assert composite_score never exceeds 1.0 / never goes negative, (b) generate identical strings and assert phonetic_similarity == 1.0, (c) generate random per-matter weights and assert verdict respects band boundaries. For extractor: hypothesis-generate line strings with random INID-code-shaped substrings and assert extract_markers_from_line never raises and only emits keys in the allowlist.

passthrough_p2.claim: There is no end-to-end test from 'drop a PDF in storage' to 'trademark rows appear in DB' using a real (or synthetically-non-trivial) PDF. test_ingest_atomic monkeypatches PDFProcessor with a `FakeProcessor` that yields canned dicts, which exercises only the orchestration layer — never the actual parser→mapper→DB integration. The mapper, _resolve_logo_path coupling, and section_to_trademark conversion are individually covered, but the contract between extractor and mapper is not.
passthrough_p2.evidence: test_ingest_atomic.py:101-108 `class FakeProcessor: def extract_records(self, *a, **k): yield from sections` skips the real parse path entirely. The /input/ directory has 8 real gazette PDFs (A_T1_2026 through B_T4_2026) but none are referenced by any test. smoke_ingest.py exists as a manual script but is not a pytest target. A schema-shape mismatch between `extract_records` output and `section_to_trademark` input would not be caught.
passthrough_p2.suggested_fix: Add tests/integration/test_ingest_real_pdf.py: copy a small representative gazette (or build a 1-page synthetic PDF with 1-2 sections matching real NOIP layout) into tests/fixtures/, run the full ingest_pdf with REAL PDFProcessor (no monkeypatch), and assert (a) rows in DB have plausible application_number / mark_sample / nice_classes, (b) logo_path resolves to a real PNG when one exists, (c) ingest completes without error. Mark slow/integration so it can be skipped in fast-loop runs but included in CI.

passthrough_p2.claim: The wordmark backfill UPDATE joins solely on `application_number` with no `record_type` predicate. The companion script `enrich_from_per_pdf_csvs.py:199-211` IS record-type-scoped — and its docstring explicitly warns the same app number can collide across types ('the A_T<n> application becomes the parent of a B_T<n+k> certificate').
passthrough_p2.evidence:
```
UPDATE trademarks t
SET mark_sample = idx.mark_sample
FROM tm_name_index idx
WHERE t.application_number = idx.application_number
  AND t.application_number IS NOT NULL
  AND t.application_number <> ''
  AND (t.mark_sample IS NULL OR t.mark_sample = '')
```
Compare to `enrich_from_per_pdf_csvs.py`:
```
AND t.record_type = %s
```
A `B_domestic` row whose `application_number` was preserved through to grant (the cert-issued path) will accept the A-file wordmark even though `tm_name_index` is by definition only A-file filings. In most cases this is the correct mark — but it's an unverified assumption, not a guard.
passthrough_p2.suggested_fix: Add `AND t.record_type = 'A'` to the WHERE clause (or normalize: every row in `tm_name_index` IS an A-file, so the right-side join inherits the type). Re-run audit and confirm the cross-type case count is what was intended.

passthrough_p2.claim: The loader for `tm_name_index` does not filter Excel error tokens (#N/A, #REF!, #VALUE!, etc.) even though the sister script `enrich_from_per_pdf_csvs.py:75-85` defines exactly this frozen set for the same data path. If NOIP ever ships a CSV with a propagated VLOOKUP error in the (540) column, it loads cleanly into the reference table and then propagates into `trademarks.mark_sample` via `enrich_mark_samples.py`.
passthrough_p2.evidence: load_tm_name_index.py only checks `if not mark: skipped_no_mark += 1; continue` — any non-empty string passes. PR #48's own commit message documents finding 12 such tokens in `csv/B_T1_2026.csv` (8x #N/A, 4x #NAME?), so the upstream-data risk is empirically present. enrich_from_per_pdf_csvs.py:159 has `if mark in _EXCEL_ERRORS: continue` — same check missing here.
passthrough_p2.suggested_fix: Import or duplicate the `_EXCEL_ERRORS` frozenset into `load_tm_name_index.py` and skip rows whose mark token matches. Add a count-of-skipped log line alongside the existing `skipped_no_mark` counter.

passthrough_p2.claim: The rate limiter keys on `get_remote_address` which reads `request.client.host`. Behind any reverse proxy (Nginx, ALB, Cloudflare), every request appears to come from the proxy IP, making per-user rate limiting effectively a single shared bucket — and behind the public internet without a proxy, header-spoofed `X-Forwarded-For` is unhonored, so the limiter can be defeated either way.
passthrough_p2.evidence:
```
return Limiter(
    key_func=get_remote_address,
    ...
)
```
`slowapi.util.get_remote_address` returns `request.client.host` directly — no `X-Forwarded-For` parsing. No `trusted_proxies` config exists. SECURITY.md:78 acknowledges 'Rate limiting is per-IP only — trivially bypassed via header spoofing if the load balancer doesn't supply X-Forwarded-For.'
passthrough_p2.suggested_fix: Either: (a) switch `key_func` to a custom resolver that reads `X-Forwarded-For` and trusts only the configured proxy CIDR; or (b) once auth is universally required, key by `user.id` instead of IP. For unauthenticated routes, do (a). Also tighten the per-endpoint limit on the export-like routes (`/trademarks?limit=500`, `/search?limit=200`) — 120/min × 500 rows = 60k rows/min trivially dumps the corpus in under a minute.

passthrough_p2.claim: The backfill never validates that the wordmark coming from `tm_name_index` looks like a wordmark. There is no length cap, no Vienna-code shape check, no marker-leakage check. A malicious or corrupt source CSV could write arbitrary text (megabyte-long strings, embedded INID markers, control characters) into `mark_sample`, which is then served unfiltered to the API and rendered in the UI.
passthrough_p2.evidence: enrich_mark_samples.py performs a raw `SET mark_sample = idx.mark_sample` — no `LENGTH(idx.mark_sample) < N`, no `idx.mark_sample !~ '\(\d{3}\)'` to catch marker leakage that `audit_fields.check_marker_leakage_in_mark_sample()` is supposed to flag. The audit script checks this *after* the fact (baseline=0); a one-off bad load won't be caught until someone re-runs the audit.
passthrough_p2.suggested_fix: Add a WHERE-side validation in the UPDATE: e.g., `AND char_length(idx.mark_sample) < 1024 AND idx.mark_sample !~ '\(\d{3}\)'`. Or pre-filter on insert in `load_tm_name_index.py`. Run `audit_fields.py` as a post-commit step in any CI that exercises enrichment.

passthrough_p3.claim: Successful login does not bump `token_version`, so a prior valid access token from before login still validates against the user record.
passthrough_p3.evidence: `login()` reads the user, verifies password, issues new tokens — but never touches `User.token_version`. If an attacker pre-authentication had any access token or refresh token from the same account (e.g., from a recent session they never logged out of, or from a hijacked refresh cookie they want to keep), it remains usable post-login. Logout-all bumps `tv`; login does not.
passthrough_p3.suggested_fix: Optional defense-in-depth: bump `token_version` on each fresh password authentication. This invalidates any prior outstanding session — at the cost of forcing the user's other tabs/devices to re-login (which is often actually desired UX after a password-entry event).

passthrough_p3.claim: `cors_origin_list` accepts any string the operator passes via `TM_CORS_ORIGINS`, including the literal `*`. Combined with `allow_credentials=True`, a misconfigured `TM_CORS_ORIGINS=*` would silently produce a fully-open credentialed CORS policy.
passthrough_p3.evidence: `cors_origin_list` just splits the env var by comma; no validation rejects '*' or wildcards. Starlette's CORSMiddleware will then echo the request Origin (with `allow_credentials=True`) for any origin, enabling cross-site credentialed reads of every endpoint. Settings has a field_validator that hard-fails the dev secret_key in prod (good) — the same belt-and-braces would suit cors_origins.
passthrough_p3.suggested_fix: Add a `@field_validator('cors_origins')` that raises if `*` appears OR if `is_production` is true and the list contains http:// origins. Likewise reject blank-only lists in production.

passthrough_p3.claim: Audit scripts create a SQLAlchemy `create_engine` per query / per PDF and never call `engine.dispose()`. Each engine owns a connection pool; running the audit across all 8 gazettes leaks 8 pools' worth of idle connections.
passthrough_p3.evidence: audit_logos.py `_db_logo_status`: `engine = create_engine(settings.database_url_sync, future=True); with engine.connect() as conn: ...` — connection is closed, engine is NOT disposed. Called once per PDF inside `audit()` loop. audit_fields.py `_conn()`: same pattern, called 8× per `main()` (once per check function). Each engine holds 5 connections by default.
passthrough_p3.suggested_fix: Hoist `engine = create_engine(...)` to module scope (or to `main()`) and reuse it across calls; or call `engine.dispose()` in a `finally:` block. Same fix in both files.

passthrough_p3.claim: mypy `exclude = "(alembic/|scripts/|tm_extractor/|image_extractor/)"` skips the scripts/ directory, so new loaders like `scripts/load_tm_name_index.py`, `scripts/enrich_mark_samples.py`, `scripts/enrich_from_per_pdf_csvs.py` get no type checking despite holding real DB-write logic. CI passes even if a script breaks an import or annotation.
passthrough_p3.evidence: pyproject.toml `[tool.mypy]` exclude regex covers scripts/. The new PR #48 family of loaders ingest 770k rows of NOIP data — exactly the place a type bug (wrong arg order on execute_values, wrong column name in tuple) hurts most. mypy currently silent on all of them.
passthrough_p3.suggested_fix: Drop `scripts/` from the exclude regex and accept the migration cost (most scripts already have annotations). At minimum, type-check the loaders that hit the DB. Alembic/versions/ stays excluded — auto-generated; that's a fair exemption.

passthrough_p3.claim: Free-text `q`, `applicant`, and `ip_agency` are interpolated directly into an `ILIKE '%{val}%'` pattern without escaping `%` or `_`. Not a SQL injection (parameterised), but a user typing `100%` matches any string containing `100`, and `_` becomes a single-char wildcard.
passthrough_p3.evidence: `like = f"%{q.lower()}%"` then `Trademark.application_number.ilike(like)`. SQLAlchemy parameterises the value but Postgres still interprets `%` and `_` as LIKE wildcards. So `q="a_b"` matches "a1b", "aXb", etc. — surprising for a literal-search feature labelled 'text' in the UI.
passthrough_p3.suggested_fix: Escape with `q.replace('\\', '\\\\').replace('%', '\\%').replace('_', '\\_')` then add `.ilike(like, escape='\\')`. Same for applicant + ip_agency filters.

passthrough_p3.claim: Filename routing regexes only match single-digit T numbers — `B_T10_2026_madrid.csv` would be silently skipped once NOIP publishes a 10th issue in a year.
passthrough_p3.evidence: `re.compile(r"^B_T\d_\d{4}_madrid\.csv$")` — `\d` matches exactly one digit. Three patterns all use the same `\d`. NOIP currently publishes 4 issues/year so the cap is academic, but the failure mode (silent skip in `_classify`) means a future T10 file produces zero enrichment with no error.
passthrough_p3.suggested_fix: Change `\d` → `\d+` in all three patterns. Or, if T must stay 1-2 digits, `\d{1,2}`.

passthrough_p3.claim: `WatchQuery(**w.query)` on every watchlist read will raise pydantic.ValidationError if the stored JSONB has a field that's been since removed from the model or has an incompatible value (e.g. an old `mode` value).
passthrough_p3.evidence: `_to_out` does `query=WatchQuery(**w.query)` with no error handling. `watchlist_findings` also does `WatchQuery(**w.query)`. With pydantic v2's default `extra='ignore'` unknown fields wouldn't raise, but a type mismatch (e.g. old watchlist where `threshold` was an int) would. The model has no migration story.
passthrough_p3.suggested_fix: Wrap in try/except ValidationError, falling back to a default WatchQuery and logging the offending row. Or add a migration step that validates + rewrites legacy JSONB on read.

passthrough_p3.claim: Several .map() loops use `key={i}` (array index) as the React key. Safe today because lists are static, but a refactor that reorders/inserts mid-list will break component state.
passthrough_p3.evidence:
landing page.tsx:341,389,462 — `bodyParas.map((p, i) =>

...)`. landing page.tsx:363 — opp-cal-row uses `key={i}`. compare/page.tsx:423 — `cells.slice(0, n).map((cell, i) =>

...)`. detail/timeline.tsx:24 — `events.map((e, i) =>
  • ...)`. The timeline one is most concerning because events ARE dynamic by mark.
passthrough_p3.suggested_fix: Use stable identifiers: paragraph text hash (or just `key={p.slice(0,20)}`), `event.date + event.kind` for timeline events, etc.

passthrough_p3.claim: Every cold visit to /login fires a `/api/v1/auth/refresh` POST even when no refresh cookie can plausibly exist.
passthrough_p3.evidence: auth-context.tsx lines 38-50 always run `refresh()` on mount with no skip condition. login/layout.tsx wraps `/login` in `<AuthProvider>` (its header even claims provider doesn't hit /auth/me — but it does call /auth/refresh, same round-trip). lib/auth.ts:65-77 confirms refresh() hits the network unconditionally. Result: every anonymous landing on the login page pays a 401 round-trip before the form is interactive.
passthrough_p3.suggested_fix: Read `document.cookie` for the existence of the refresh cookie name (set httpOnly=false on a sentinel cookie if needed) and skip refresh() when it's absent. Or split AuthProvider into a 'check' provider only used on (app)/ pages and let /login skip it entirely.

passthrough_p3.claim: Table rows have `cursor-pointer` styling but no actual row onClick — only the inner Link works. Misleads users that any cell is clickable.
passthrough_p3.evidence: results-table.tsx line 41: `<tr className={'... cursor-pointer'}>`. No onClick handler on the tr. Inner `<Link>` elements are at columns 'Mark' and 'Name/Applicant' only. Hovering an empty cell shows the pointer cursor but nothing happens on click.
passthrough_p3.suggested_fix: Either remove `cursor-pointer` from the row and only apply it to actual interactive cells, OR add an onClick to navigate to /marks/<id> with proper a11y (role='link' + keyboard nav) and event delegation that excludes the checkbox cell.

passthrough_p3.claim: users.token_version is documented as `Postgres BIGINT so we never wrap` but is actually declared as `Integer` (sa.Integer / Postgres int4). Doc/code mismatch only; INTEGER's 2.1B range will not wrap in practice.
passthrough_p3.evidence: models.py:291-293 docstring: 'Stored as Postgres BIGINT so we never wrap'. Actual column at L293: `token_version: Mapped[int] = mapped_column(Integer, ...)`. Migration 0010 L62: `sa.Column("token_version", sa.Integer(), nullable=False, server_default="0")`. Postgres `\d users` confirms `token_version | integer`.
passthrough_p3.suggested_fix: Either update the model + migration to `BIGINT` to match the docstring, or update the docstring to say 'INTEGER (2B range is plenty)'. The latter is simpler and equally correct.

passthrough_p3.claim: After updating ~42k trademarks rows (touching mark_sample, which is covered by the GIN trigram index), the script does not run `ANALYZE trademarks`. Planner stats lag until autovacuum picks it up.
passthrough_p3.evidence: enrich_mark_samples.py:86-98 executes the UPDATE and commits; no `cur.execute('ANALYZE trademarks')`. pg_stat_user_tables shows last_autoanalyze on trademarks ran 2026-05-28 11:17, which presumably captured it, but on production loads autovacuum_analyze_threshold may delay this. The trigram GIN re-builds incrementally per UPDATE; only ANALYZE refreshes histogram for planning.
passthrough_p3.suggested_fix: After `conn.commit()` add `cur.execute('ANALYZE trademarks')`. Same recommendation for load_tm_name_index.py after the COPY/bulk insert.

passthrough_p3.claim: Four CVEs are ignored in pip-audit and Trivy with hand-written rationale comments but no automated expiry. When python-multipart 0.0.22 / starlette 1.0.1 / jaraco.context 6.1.0 / wheel 0.46.2 ship, nothing will surface that fact — the ignores are durable and CI stays quiet.
passthrough_p3.evidence: ci.yml:144-148 hardcodes `--ignore-vuln CVE-2026-24486 CVE-2026-40347 CVE-2026-42561 PYSEC-2026-161`. .trivyignore lines 12-14 mirror three of them. Both files carry comments like 'needs unreleased 0.0.22' but no `# expires: 2026-07-01` and no scheduled job to re-evaluate.
passthrough_p3.suggested_fix: Add a monthly scheduled workflow that runs `pip-audit` WITHOUT the ignores and opens an issue if any of the listed CVEs no longer matches a current dep version. Trivy supports `expiredAt:` in ignore files — adopt it. Pair the .trivyignore line with a Dependabot label that opens a tracking issue.

passthrough_p3.claim: TM_UPLOAD_DIR defaults to `/tmp/tm_uploads` inside the container. `/tmp` survives container restart but not container recreate (image redeploy). There is no VOLUME directive declaring it as a persistent mount point, and DEPLOYMENT.md only mentions mounting it in the `docker run` example — k8s/compose users have no machine-readable hint.
passthrough_p3.evidence: Dockerfile has no `VOLUME /tmp/tm_uploads` or `VOLUME /srv/backend/uploads` declaration. settings.py:45 default `Path('/tmp/tm_uploads')`. Routes/gazettes.py:68 creates it lazily. DEPLOYMENT.md:53-64 mentions `-v /tmp/tm_uploads:/tmp/tm_uploads:rw` but no docker-compose service example. SECURITY.md:71 acknowledges the volume's loss-on-removal risk for pgdata; same risk applies here, undocumented.
passthrough_p3.suggested_fix: (a) Add `VOLUME ['/srv/backend/uploads']` to the Dockerfile and change the default to `/srv/backend/uploads` so the contract is self-documenting; (b) extend DEPLOYMENT.md with a compose service example wiring a named volume. (c) Note: PDFs are recoverable by re-upload (SHA dedup), so this is operational annoyance not data-loss — hence P3.

passthrough_p3.claim: Dependabot is configured to schedule pip / npm / actions checks MONTHLY. Security advisories still trigger immediate PRs (Dependabot's default), so the practical risk is low — but a monthly cadence for routine bumps means transitive minor-version regressions (e.g. a regex CVE in a Pydantic minor) wait up to 30 days for non-security categorized advisories.
passthrough_p3.evidence: dependabot.yml:23 `interval: 'monthly'` (and lines 52, 87 — all three ecosystems). Industry baseline for a tool with security-sensitive deps (auth, file upload, PDF parsing) is weekly. The dependabot config helpfully calls out that security PRs aren't delayed (lines 42-46), which is correct, but version-update PRs for minor bumps still are.
passthrough_p3.suggested_fix: Move to `interval: 'weekly'` for pip + npm. Keep github-actions monthly (less code, lower regression risk). Grouped PRs (already configured) keep the noise manageable.

passthrough_p3.claim: `/metrics` is exposed with no authentication and enabled by default, leaking internal endpoint paths and traffic patterns to anyone who can reach the API.
passthrough_p3.evidence: `settings.enable_prometheus: bool = True` (settings.py:61) and `Instrumentator(...).expose(app, endpoint='/metrics', include_in_schema=False)` is mounted with no `Depends(require_admin)`. `include_in_schema=False` only hides it from OpenAPI — it does not gate access. Anyone who knows the URL gets `http_requests_total{handler='/api/v1/marks/{id}', status='404'}` etc., which reveals route shape, traffic volume, and error rates to an attacker. The metrics middleware groups by handler+status (`should_group_status_codes=True`), which is good for cardinality but still leaks the endpoint inventory.
passthrough_p3.suggested_fix: Either restrict by network (only allow `/metrics` from the Prometheus scrape pod's IP) or require basic-auth (`PROM_METRICS_BASIC_AUTH` env). The prometheus-fastapi-instrumentator exposes an `auth` callable for this. Alternatively, mount `/metrics` only when `TM_ENV != 'production'` AND a dedicated `enable_public_metrics` flag is set.

passthrough_p3.claim: No SQLAlchemy query echo, no `pg_stat_statements` enablement, no slow-query log: operators cannot find which queries are slow without re-running with `echo=True`.
passthrough_p3.evidence: `_engine_kwargs['echo'] = False` (line 44) is hardcoded with no env flag. `grep -rn 'echo=\|SQL_ECHO\|log_statement\|pg_stat_statements' app/backend` returns only the `echo=False` line. `postgres:16-alpine` in `docker-compose.yml` is launched with no `command:` overrides, so `log_min_duration_statement` is at its default (off). The 30s `statement_timeout` (session.py:48) caps damage from a runaway query but tells nothing about the 25s queries that complete and slowly degrade UX.
passthrough_p3.suggested_fix: Add `TM_DB_ECHO: bool = False` to Settings and wire it to `_engine_kwargs['echo']`. In compose, add `command: ['postgres', '-c', 'log_min_duration_statement=500', '-c', 'shared_preload_libraries=pg_stat_statements']` to capture slow queries to the postgres logs. Add an admin endpoint or `psql` runbook to read `pg_stat_statements`.

passthrough_p3.claim: The container HEALTHCHECK probes `/health` (liveness only) instead of `/health/ready`, so when Postgres or Redis is down the container is marked healthy and continues receiving traffic.
passthrough_p3.evidence: Dockerfile: `HEALTHCHECK ... CMD curl --fail --silent http://localhost:8000/health || exit 1`. The `/health` endpoint (main.py:159-162) just returns `{'status': 'ok'}` unconditionally. `/health/ready` (lines 164-188) is the one that actually probes DB and Redis, but is not used by Docker. Production orchestrators (k8s liveness vs readiness) would need to be configured explicitly to differentiate, and the Dockerfile sets the precedent for `docker run` and Docker Swarm users who'll inherit the wrong default.
passthrough_p3.suggested_fix: Keep `/health` as the liveness probe in the Dockerfile HEALTHCHECK (correct — restarts on a wedged process), but document in `DEPLOYMENT.md` that orchestrators MUST configure `readinessProbe.httpGet.path = /health/ready` separately so degraded states drain traffic. Optionally add a `compose.override.yml` example showing both probes for non-k8s deployments.

passthrough_p3.claim: Visual baseline coverage gap: 4 of 15 routes have no baseline — /gazettes (legacy in-app gazettes page), /dev/showcase (component showcase, may be intentional), /marks/[id] (depends on seeded data, intentional), /trademarks/[id] (legacy detail page, may be redirect-only). A new design regression on /gazettes would not be caught by visual tests.
passthrough_p3.evidence: Routes inventory (find -name page.tsx): landing, login, docs, pricing, coverage, today, watchlists, search, gazettes, compare, docs/[slug], marks/[id], admin/gazettes, trademarks/[id], dev/showcase. Baselines under visual.spec.ts-snapshots: 10 files covering 10 routes (landing, today, pricing, coverage, docs-getting-started, search-empty, compare-empty, admin-gazettes, watchlists, login). The 4 uncovered routes are deterministic except marks/[id] and trademarks/[id] which depend on data.
passthrough_p3.suggested_fix: Add visual baselines for /gazettes (chrome-only, no data) and /dev/showcase. Document in visual.spec.ts comment why /marks/[id] + /trademarks/[id] are excluded (data-dependent) so future devs don't add them and create flakes.

passthrough_p3.claim: Uploaded gazette PDFs accumulate in `TM_UPLOAD_DIR` (default `/tmp/tm_uploads`) with no TTL or retention policy. Each is 10-100 MB; over time this fills disk and complicates GDPR-style data-subject deletion (the source PDF contains all the applicant PII).
passthrough_p3.evidence: upload_gazette renames the temp file to `{digest[:16]}_{safe_name}.pdf` and stores `storage_path` permanently. No DELETE handler. `_purge_trademarks` only removes DB rows, never the file. The dedup-by-sha256 path even keeps reuploads idempotent — but never expires.
passthrough_p3.suggested_fix: Define a retention policy in `Settings` (e.g. `TM_UPLOAD_RETENTION_DAYS`) and add a background job (RQ cron, or a simple `find -mtime +N -delete` cron) that purges files older than that AND nulls/anonymises the `gazettes.storage_path`. Document the policy alongside the GDPR section in SECURITY.md.

original.claim: Watchlist.owner_id stores a stringified user UUID in `String(255)` with no foreign key to `users.id`. When a user is deleted (or `users` is rebuilt), watchlists become orphaned and `_assert_owned` will allow nobody (or, worse, an attacker who creates a user whose row gets the recycled UUID — defended only by UUID collision impossibility).

refuted_p0p1.refutation: The structural observation is accurate — `Watchlist.owner_id` (models.py:123) and `Gazette.uploaded_by` (models.py:92) are `String(255)` with no FK to `users.id`, and `routes/watchlists.py:106` stores `owner_id=user.id` (a stringified UUID). However the claimed impact does not hold: (1) There is NO user-delete endpoint in the entire backend — `grep router.delete` returns only the watchlist delete route. So orphaning via normal app use cannot occur. (2) Even if a user were deleted by direct DB manipulation, the `_assert_owned` check at line 167 (`if w.owner_id and w.owner_id != user.id`) would correctly 403 every other user because `w.owner_id` is still a non-null stringified UUID that matches nobody — i.e., "allow nobody" is the safe-failure behavior, not a vulnerability. The finding's "allow nobody (or worse...)" framing inverts the actual semantics: the orphan stays locked, it doesn't open up. (3) The "attacker creates a user with recycled UUID" vector requires a UUIDv4 collision (cryptographically impossible) AND the attacker would need to choose their own UUID, which `User.id` defaults via server-side `uuid.uuid4()` (models.py:275) — attackers can't pick it via the auth registration path. The finding itself concedes "defended only by UUID collision impossibility," which is in fact a complete defense for any real-world adversary. Adding the FK is reasonable schema hygiene (defense-in-depth, prevents typo'd manual inserts, enables ON DELETE SET NULL semantics for future delete endpoints), but it is P3 at most — not a P0 data-integrity issue with material risk to existing users or production data.

original.claim: submission_date and registration_date_151 are queried in WHERE/range filters but neither has an index. Both queries seq-scan trademarks.

refuted_p0p1.refutation: Partially refuted. The registration_date_151 half is real: app/backend/api/routes/_filters.py:141-144 wires grant_date_from/to to Trademark.registration_date_151 with no backing index (verified across all 11 alembic migrations — only ix_trademarks_* indexes exist on gazette_id, record_type, application/certificate/madrid numbers, country_code, city, applicant_type, applicant_name, ip_agency, month, year). However, the submission_date half is fabricated: a repo-wide grep finds zero WHERE clauses on Trademark.submission_date. Every use in app/backend/api/routes/ (marks.py:106-107, 444) is an attribute read on an already PK-fetched row, never a filter. The finding's cited EXPLAIN against `WHERE submission_date >= ...` is a synthetic query not issued anywhere in the application. The "timeline/today routes also hit these dates" claim is also wrong — today.py and search.py filter and order on publication_date_441, not submission_date or registration_date_151 (publication_date_441 is itself unindexed, but that's a different finding the author didn't raise). Additionally, the cited 12.8ms/20.8ms EXPLAIN numbers don't justify P1 today — they're sub-30ms and not user-perceptible. The scaling argument to 750k rows is plausible but speculative (no booked timeline for 16x growth). At best this should be a P2/P3 single-column finding on registration_date_151 alone, with the submission_date claim dropped entirely. Because the finding bundles a real issue with a fabricated one and uses synthetic EXPLAIN evidence for a query that doesn't exist, the evidence fails adversarial review as written.

original.claim: PR #48's wordmark backfill landed two production scripts that mutate ~42k trademark rows, yet there are no pytest assertions on either loader or enrichment behavior. The script is the canonical mechanism to remediate ~100% of A-record mark_sample NULLs, but neither dry-run output, idempotency, nor the JOIN logic is verified.

refuted_p0p1.refutation: The evidence is factually accurate — both scripts exist with the cited behaviors (BOM-strip via utf-8-sig, %y date parsing, ON CONFLICT DO UPDATE, 5000-row batches, skip counters, `(mark_sample IS NULL OR = '')` guard), and grepping app/backend/tests/ returns zero matches for either script name. However, the P1 severity and the framing as a data-pipeline-coverage risk do not survive scrutiny. (1) The same pattern holds for every script in app/backend/scripts/ — backfill_logo_paths, audit_logos, audit_fields, audit_enrichment, enrich_from_per_pdf_csvs, smoke_ingest, create_user all lack pytest coverage. The absence of tests for these new scripts is consistent with project convention, not a regression introduced by PR #48. (2) These are one-shot operator-run remediation scripts, not request-path code; both default to --dry-run (rollback) and require an explicit --execute flag. (3) A dedicated audit_enrichment.py script (151 lines, also in the PR) provides 10-row DB↔CSV spot-checking plus a coverage-gap report — a direct functional mitigation that the finding did not credit. (4) The cited harm ("inverting the guard would overwrite real (540) data") is purely hypothetical — there is no evidence of a typo in the current code, and dry-run + audit_enrichment.py would catch such a regression at run time. (5) PR #48 was already executed and verified live (per the commit message: /search?q=MARVIS, Today digest opposition windows), with documented residual coverage figures that match the audit script's output. Adding tests would be a reasonable maintainability improvement (P3), but characterizing this as a P1 data-pipeline risk overstates the actual exposure given the operator-controlled invocation, dry-run default, and existing audit tooling.
